---
title: "Population"
description: "Canada's population trends, growth components, and provincial breakdown"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import single_line
```

## Canada's Population Over Time

Canada's population has grown steadily since the mid-20th century. This chart shows quarterly population estimates for Canada as a whole.

```{python}
#| echo: false
df = pd.read_csv("data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv", parse_dates=["date"])

# National total — Canada
canada = df[df["geography"] == "Canada"].copy()

fig = single_line(canada, "date", "population", title="", yaxis_title="Population",
                  rangeslider=True, yaxis_tickformat=",",
                  customdata=canada["date"].dt.quarter,
                  hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%Y} Q%{customdata}</b><br>Population: %{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>",
                  source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0009-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv')}")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Population by Province and Territory

Select provinces to compare using the legend. Click a name to toggle it; double-click to isolate one province.

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Provincial breakdown
provinces = df[df["geography"] != "Canada"].copy()

# Get latest date for sorting
latest = provinces.groupby("geography")["population"].last().sort_values(ascending=False)
top_provinces = latest.head(10).index.tolist()

# Color palette
colors = [
    "#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd",
    "#8c564b", "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf",
    "#aec7e8", "#ffbb78", "#98df8a", "#ff9896", "#c5b0d5",
]

fig = go.Figure()
for i, geo in enumerate(top_provinces):
    subset = provinces[provinces["geography"] == geo]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=subset["date"],
        y=subset["population"],
        name=geo,
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=colors[i % len(colors)], width=2),
        hovertemplate=f"<b>{geo}</b><br>%{{x|%Y}} Q%{{customdata}}: %{{y:,.0f}}<extra></extra>",
        customdata=subset["date"].dt.quarter,
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="",
    yaxis_title="Population",
    yaxis_tickformat=",",
    plot_bgcolor="white",
    xaxis=dict(
        gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
        rangeselector=dict(
            buttons=[
                dict(count=1, label="1Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=2, label="2Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=50, label="50Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All"),
            ],
            x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01,
        ),
        rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5"),
        range=["1999-01-01", provinces["date"].max()],
    ),
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
    hovermode="x unified",
    legend=dict(
        orientation="v", yanchor="top", y=1,
        xanchor="left", x=1.02,
        itemclick="toggle", itemdoubleclick="toggleothers",
    ),
    margin=dict(b=110, t=40, r=160),
    annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0009-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv')}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.30,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"),
    )],
)
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Population Growth Rate

Year-over-year percentage change in Canada's quarterly population. This is derived from the same quarterly estimates shown above — each quarter is compared to the same quarter one year earlier.

```{python}
#| echo: false
# Compute YoY growth rate for Canada
canada_growth = df[df["geography"] == "Canada"].copy()
canada_growth = canada_growth.sort_values("date")
canada_growth["pop_yoy_pct"] = canada_growth["population"].pct_change(periods=4) * 100
canada_growth = canada_growth.dropna(subset=["pop_yoy_pct"])

fig = single_line(canada_growth, "date", "pop_yoy_pct", title="",
                  yaxis_title="Year-over-Year Growth (%)", rangeslider=True,
                  customdata=canada_growth["date"].dt.quarter,
                  hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%Y} Q%{customdata}</b><br>Growth: %{y:.2f}%<extra></extra>",
                  source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0009-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv')}")
fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Components of Population Change

Births, deaths, immigration, and emigration — the drivers behind Canada's population growth. Data from Statistics Canada's components of population growth table (quarterly estimates).

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os

components_path = "data/population/statcan_population_components.csv"
if os.path.exists(components_path):
    comp = pd.read_csv(components_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    comp = comp[comp["geography"] == "Canada"].copy()

    # Keep the main components for clarity
    main_components = [
        "Births",
        "Deaths",
        "Immigrants",
        "Emigrants",
        "Net interprovincial migration",
        "Non-permanent residents",
    ]
    # Try matching — StatCan component names may vary slightly
    available = comp["component"].unique()
    keep = [c for c in available if any(m.lower() in c.lower() for m in main_components)]
    if not keep:
        # Fallback: show top components by absolute value
        top = comp.groupby("component")["value"].apply(lambda x: x.abs().mean()).nlargest(6)
        keep = top.index.tolist()

    comp_filtered = comp[comp["component"].isin(keep)]

    colors_map = {
        "Births": "#2ca02c",
        "Deaths": "#d62728",
        "Immigrants": "#1f77b4",
        "Emigrants": "#ff7f0e",
    }

    fig = go.Figure()
    for component_name in keep:
        subset = comp_filtered[comp_filtered["component"] == component_name].sort_values("date")
        color = next((v for k, v in colors_map.items() if k.lower() in component_name.lower()), None)
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=subset["date"],
            y=subset["value"],
            name=component_name,
            mode="lines",
            line=dict(color=color, width=2) if color else dict(width=2),
            hovertemplate=f"<b>{component_name}</b><br>%{{x|%Y}} Q%{{customdata}}: %{{y:,.0f}}<extra></extra>",
            customdata=subset["date"].dt.quarter,
        ))

    fig.update_layout(
        xaxis_title="",
        yaxis_title="Persons (quarterly)",
        yaxis_tickformat=",",
        plot_bgcolor="white",
        xaxis=dict(
            gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            rangeselector=dict(
                buttons=[
                    dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                    dict(step="all", label="All"),
                ],
                x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01,
            ),
            rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5"),
            range=["1999-01-01", comp["date"].max()],
        ),
        yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        hovermode="x unified",
        legend=dict(
            orientation="v", yanchor="top", y=1,
            xanchor="left", x=1.02,
            itemclick="toggle", itemdoubleclick="toggleothers",
        ),
        margin=dict(b=110, t=40, r=200),
        annotations=[dict(
            text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0014-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(components_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.30,
            showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"),
        )],
    )
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    from IPython.display import Markdown
    display(Markdown("*Population components data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch this dataset.*"))
```

## Non-Permanent Residents

The number of non-permanent residents — people in Canada on work or study permits, or as asylum claimants and protected persons — has risen sharply in recent years. It is central to current debates about the pace of population growth, housing demand, and the capacity of public services. The line shows the total across all permit types.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
npr_path = "data/population/statcan_temp_residents.csv"
if os.path.exists(npr_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(npr_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "npr_count", title="",
                      yaxis_title="Non-permanent residents", yaxis_tickformat=",",
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 17-10-0121-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(npr_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Non-permanent residents data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Diversity by City

Where Canada's visible-minority communities live, by metropolitan area (2021 Census). **Use the dropdown to switch between groups**, and zoom and pan to your area. This is a descriptive picture of residential geography — "visible minority" is Statistics Canada's term, and it is a separate concept from Indigenous identity. There is no "good" or "bad" reading here; it simply shows where people live. The **"Not a visible minority"** option is StatCan's residual category — it includes white *and* Indigenous people; the census has no distinct "White" group.

For the full **neighbourhood-level** view — every census tract, where you can zoom right to your own street — open the dedicated map (kept on its own page so this one stays fast to load):

**[→ Diversity by neighbourhood (census-tract map)](neighbourhoods.qmd)**

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os, json
from pipeline.charts import choropleth_groups_map
eth_geo = "data/geo/cma_2021.geojson"
eth_path = "data/geo/statcan_cma_ethnicity.csv"
if os.path.exists(eth_geo) and os.path.exists(eth_path):
    geo = json.load(open(eth_geo))
    eth = pd.read_csv(eth_path, dtype={"cmauid": str})
    groups = [("all_vm", "All visible minorities"), ("south_asian", "South Asian"),
              ("chinese", "Chinese"), ("black", "Black"), ("filipino", "Filipino"),
              ("arab", "Arab"), ("latin_american", "Latin American"),
              ("southeast_asian", "Southeast Asian"), ("west_asian", "West Asian"),
              ("korean", "Korean"), ("japanese", "Japanese"),
              ("not_vm", "Not a visible minority")]
    fig = choropleth_groups_map(geo, eth, "cmauid", groups, name_col="name", colorscale="Purples",
        source_note="Source: 2021 Census (Statistics Canada). 'Visible minority' is StatCan's term; 'Not a visible minority' includes white and Indigenous people. Descriptive only.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Diversity map not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## How the Diversity of Canada Has Changed

Canada's visible-minority population has grown from roughly one in eight people in the early 2000s to more than one in four. This chart traces each group's share across recent censuses. **Use the dropdown to switch geography** — Canada, a province, or a major city — and click legend entries to add or remove groups (the "Not a visible minority" majority starts hidden so the smaller groups are legible; click it on to see the full composition).

A few notes for honest reading: shares here are of the **population aged 15 and over** (the basis of the only consistent multi-census table), so they differ slightly from the all-ages maps above; **2011** was the *voluntary* National Household Survey rather than the mandatory census, so its point is flagged and less directly comparable; and sub-group definitions have shifted modestly over the decades, so the **"All visible minorities"** total is the most comparable line.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
from pipeline.charts import history_lines, VM_GROUP_COLORS
vmh_path = "data/geo/statcan_vm_history.csv"
if os.path.exists(vmh_path):
    vmh = pd.read_csv(vmh_path)
    fig = history_lines(vmh, group_colors=VM_GROUP_COLORS,
        hidden_groups=["Not a visible minority"], thick_group="All visible minorities",
        yaxis_title="% of population (aged 15+)",
        source_note="Source: 2021 Census, Statistics Canada Table 98-10-0429-01 (visible minority by census year; population aged 15+). 2011 = voluntary National Household Survey.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Historical diversity data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## Religion by City

The religious make-up of Canada's metropolitan areas (2021 Census). **Use the dropdown to switch between groups**, and zoom and pan to your area. Religion is self-reported, and the census asks it only once a decade. Like the diversity map above, this is a descriptive picture of where people live — there is no "good" or "bad" reading. **"No religion / secular"** is now the second-largest group nationally and is included; the many Christian denominations are rolled into a single "Christian" total. This is a separate concept from ethnicity and from Indigenous identity.

For the full **neighbourhood-level** view — every census tract, where you can zoom right to your own street — open the dedicated map (kept on its own page so this one stays fast to load):

**[→ Religion by neighbourhood (census-tract map)](religion-neighbourhoods.qmd)**

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os, json
from pipeline.charts import choropleth_groups_map
rel_geo = "data/geo/cma_2021.geojson"
rel_path = "data/geo/statcan_cma_religion.csv"
if os.path.exists(rel_geo) and os.path.exists(rel_path):
    geo = json.load(open(rel_geo))
    rel = pd.read_csv(rel_path, dtype={"cmauid": str})
    groups = [("christian", "Christian"), ("no_religion", "No religion / secular"),
              ("muslim", "Muslim"), ("hindu", "Hindu"), ("sikh", "Sikh"),
              ("buddhist", "Buddhist"), ("jewish", "Jewish"),
              ("indigenous_spirituality", "Traditional Indigenous spirituality"),
              ("other_religion", "Other religions & spiritual traditions")]
    fig = choropleth_groups_map(geo, rel, "cmauid", groups, name_col="name", colorscale="Teal",
        source_note="Source: 2021 Census (Statistics Canada); % of population in private households. Religion is self-reported. Descriptive only.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Religion map not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## How Religious Affiliation Has Changed

Canada has recorded religion in its census roughly **once a decade since 1871**, so the long view is unusually deep. The chart below traces Canada's composition over 150 years: a country that was about **95% Christian** from the 1870s through the mid-20th century became, by 2021, **53% Christian** — while **"No religion / secular"** rose from **0.2% to 35%**, now the second-largest group. Muslim, Hindu, Sikh and Buddhist populations were a negligible share until first counted separately in 1981, and each has grown steadily since. Use the **Show:** selector to switch between each group's *share* of the population and the *absolute number of people* — in raw counts even Christianity grew (Canada's population rose roughly tenfold over the period), even as its share fell.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import history_lines, RELIGION_HISTORY_COLORS
rellr_path = "data/geo/statcan_religion_canada_longrun.csv"
if os.path.exists(rellr_path):
    rellr = pd.read_csv(rellr_path)
    # "Other religions" was the catch-all for smaller faiths until the world religions
    # (Muslim/Hindu/Sikh/Buddhist) were counted separately from 1981 — so show it only for
    # that earlier era (≤1971) rather than imply it still contains them afterwards.
    rellr = rellr[~((rellr["group"] == "Other religions") & (rellr["year"] > 1971))]
    fig = history_lines(rellr, group_colors=RELIGION_HISTORY_COLORS, dropdown=False,
        thick_group="Christian",
        measures=[dict(label="% of population", col="share", fmt=".1f", suffix="%",
                       ytitle="% of total population"),
                  dict(label="Number of people", col="count", fmt=",.0f", suffix="",
                       ytitle="Number of people")],
        source_note="Sources: Censuses of Canada 1871–2021 — StatCan Table 17-10-0073-01 (1871–1971), published census tabulations (1981–2001), 2011 National Household Survey, 2021 Census. Self-reported religion. World religions counted separately from 1981; “Other” shown to 1971 only; 2011 = voluntary NHS.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Long-run religion data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

For honest reading: religion is recorded once a decade (years ending in 1) on a total-population basis, and "Christian" sums all Christian denominations. This deep series is **Canada only**, and methods differ by era — **1981–2001** used the mandatory long form, **2011** the *voluntary* National Household Survey (flagged on the chart), and **2021** the mandatory census. **"Other religions" is shown only through 1971**: in that era it was the catch-all for all smaller faiths (including the then-tiny Muslim, Hindu, Sikh and Buddhist populations), but once those were counted separately from 1981 it would mean something narrower — so the line stops rather than imply continuity.

### The scale of the shift, 1981–2021

The lines above show *direction*; this ranks *magnitude*. Over four decades the Christian share fell by more than any other group's gain, while "No religion / secular" rose almost as much — together these two account for most of the change. The Muslim, Hindu and Sikh shares are smaller, but each more than quadrupled. (1981 is the first census in which the world religions were separately counted.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import change_bars
rellr_path = "data/geo/statcan_religion_canada_longrun.csv"
if os.path.exists(rellr_path):
    fig = change_bars(pd.read_csv(rellr_path), group_colors=RELIGION_HISTORY_COLORS,
        year_start=1981, year_end=2021,
        source_note="Source: Censuses of Canada, 1981 & 2021 (Statistics Canada).")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "displayModeBar": False})
else:
    print("Long-run religion data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

### By province and city, 2011 vs 2021

The series above is national. To compare places, the chart below switches between Canada, each province and territory, and the eight largest metro areas for the two most recent censuses. Shares are of the total population; the smaller groups sit near the bottom of the axis — click legend entries to isolate them.

```{python}
#| echo: false
relh_path = "data/geo/statcan_religion_history.csv"
if os.path.exists(relh_path):
    relh = pd.read_csv(relh_path)
    fig = history_lines(relh, group_colors=RELIGION_HISTORY_COLORS,
        thick_group="Christian", yaxis_title="% of population",
        source_note="Source: 2021 Census (Table 98-10-0353) + 2011 National Household Survey profiles, Statistics Canada. Self-reported religion, % of population in private households. 2011 = voluntary NHS.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Religion-over-time data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

### How the religious mix varies by city, 2021

The national story plays out very differently across the country. **Toronto and Vancouver** are among the most religiously diverse metros anywhere — sizeable Muslim, Hindu, Sikh and Buddhist populations alongside a Christian plurality — while **Montréal** remains strongly Christian (Catholic) and the West Coast is heavily secular. Each bar is one metro's full 2021 composition; the segments sum to 100%.

These are **census metropolitan areas**, not just the core city: "Toronto" is the wider metro of ~6.2 million spanning the inner GTA — Mississauga, Brampton, Markham, Vaughan and more — not the City of Toronto alone (~2.8 million). That broader footprint is part of why its Sikh and Hindu shares are so large (Brampton in particular).

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import composition_bars
relh_path = "data/geo/statcan_religion_history.csv"
if os.path.exists(relh_path):
    rc = pd.read_csv(relh_path)
    cities = rc[(rc["geo_level"] == "cma") & (rc["year"] == 2021)]
    order = cities[cities["group"] == "Christian"].sort_values("share")["geography"].tolist()
    fig = composition_bars(rc, group_colors=RELIGION_HISTORY_COLORS, year=2021,
        geographies=["Canada"] + order,
        source_note="Source: 2021 Census (Statistics Canada). Self-reported religion, % of population. Cities = census metropolitan areas, ordered by Christian share.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Religion-by-city data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## Download Data

- [Population Quarterly Estimates (CSV)](../data/population/statcan_population_quarterly.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 17-10-0009-01
- [Non-Permanent Residents (CSV)](../data/population/statcan_temp_residents.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 17-10-0121-01
- [Visible-Minority Groups by City (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_cma_ethnicity.csv) — 2021 Census (Statistics Canada)
- [Visible-Minority Groups by Neighbourhood / census tract (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_ct_ethnicity_2021.csv) — 2021 Census (Statistics Canada)
- [Visible-Minority Composition by Census Year (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_vm_history.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 98-10-0429-01 (population aged 15+)
- [Religion by City (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_cma_religion.csv) — 2021 Census (Statistics Canada)
- [Religion by Neighbourhood / census tract (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_ct_religion_2021.csv) — 2021 Census (Statistics Canada)
- [Religion by Census Year (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_religion_history.csv) — Statistics Canada 2021 Census + 2011 NHS profiles
- [Religion in Canada, 1871–2021 (CSV)](../data/geo/statcan_religion_canada_longrun.csv) — Statistics Canada censuses (Tables 17-10-0073-01, 95F0303X, major-denominations tabulations, NHS 2011, Census 2021)
